title: RL Meets Graphs: Routing Decisions Over a Structured Action Space
concept: Combining Reinforcement Learning with Graph-Structured Problems
learningObjectives: Explain why a routing decision is naturally a graph-structured action space, unlike the battery scheduler's simple discrete/continuous actions
Frame a grid-routing problem as an MDP where the state includes graph topology information and actions are edge/path choices
Explain at least one strategy for handling a large or variable-sized action space (e.g. restricting to top-k candidate paths from Dijkstra before letting an RL agent choose)
Compare a graph-algorithm-only routing solution against an RL-augmented one for a scenario with dynamic, changing conditions
whyItMatters: Mission 5's two skill folders have so far run in parallel — reinforcement learning for battery scheduling, graph algorithms for grid structure — but real grid routing decisions often need both: the graph structure defines what's even possible, while conditions change fast enough (real-time prices, live capacity, evolving contingencies) that a fixed algorithm alone can't adapt as well as a learned policy that's seen many scenarios.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's routing-decision layer — the actual convergence point of this mission's two skill folders, where graph algorithms define the feasible action space and reinforcement learning chooses adaptively within it.

problem.summary: Static routing recomputation can't learn from patterns across past scenarios.
problem.content: Dijkstra and max-flow from earlier lessons compute the best static routing for a fixed snapshot of conditions, but real conditions change continuously — prices shift, a line's available capacity fluctuates, a contingency might already be underway — and recomputing from scratch every time doesn't let the system learn from past routing decisions the way an RL agent's battery scheduler does.

motivation.summary: This is where the two halves of Mission 5 were always going to need each other.
motivation.content: This is the actual point where the two halves of Mission 5 were always going to need each other: an RL agent with no graph structure would have to learn routing from nothing, which is enormously harder than it needs to be, while a graph algorithm alone can't adapt to patterns across many scenarios the way a trained policy can.

theory.summary: Restrict the action space to Dijkstra-generated candidates, letting RL adapt within it.
theory.content: A routing MDP's state includes not just the usual demand/price/battery information but the graph's current topology and capacity state; actions are typically restricted to a manageable candidate set — e.g. the top-k lowest-loss paths from Dijkstra between the relevant source and destination — rather than every conceivable path, since the full action space of 'any possible route' is usually far too large for an RL agent to explore directly. This candidate-restriction approach is a common, practical pattern for combining classical algorithms with RL: let the classical algorithm (Dijkstra, max-flow) do what it's provably good at (finding optimal or near-optimal candidates for a static snapshot), and let the learned policy handle the part classical algorithms structurally can't — adapting a choice among those candidates based on patterns learned across many scenarios, including ones a purely algorithmic approach wouldn't know to weight differently.

visualExplanation.summary: Dijkstra generates candidates; an RL agent selects among them based on learned patterns.
visualExplanation.content: Dijkstra generates a shortlist of candidate routing paths, feeding into an RL agent that selects among them based on learned patterns across many past scenarios, contrasted with a pure graph-algorithm approach that always picks the same statically-optimal choice regardless of broader context.

interactiveDemo.summary: Compare static Dijkstra-only choices against RL-augmented choices across shifting scenarios.
interactiveDemo.content: Compare a static Dijkstra-only routing choice against an RL-augmented choice across several scenarios with shifting conditions, observing where the learned policy diverges from the static algorithm and why.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: generate candidate paths and let a policy choose among them.

independentCoding.summary: Implement k-shortest-paths and construct a scenario favoring the adaptive policy.
independentCoding.content: Implement a simple k-shortest-paths candidate generator for a routing decision, and write a scenario where a purely static Dijkstra choice would perform worse than a policy that could adapt to a pattern (e.g. a recurring evening price spike) across many similar past scenarios.

debugging.summary: Fix a candidate generator that always returns only one option (k=1).
debugging.content: A candidate-restriction implementation always includes only the single Dijkstra-optimal path (k=1), silently giving the RL agent no actual choice to learn from. Identify why this defeats the purpose of combining the two approaches and fix it to generate a genuine candidate set.

reflection.summary: A single-candidate action space defeats the purpose of the RL component.
reflection.content: If your candidate-generation step only ever produces one option, what has the RL agent actually learned to do, and why does that make the whole combination pointless?

teachBack.summary: Explain why top-k restriction is a practical compromise, not a lesser version of either approach.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone why restricting an RL agent's action space to Dijkstra's top-k candidates is a practical compromise, rather than either a lesser version of RL or an unnecessary complication of a purely graph-algorithmic approach.

miniProject.summary: Build a routing-decision module beating static top-1 Dijkstra in at least one scenario.
miniProject.content: Build a routing-decision module that generates k candidate paths for a given source-destination pair using graph algorithms, and uses a simple learned policy to choose among them based on current state, demonstrating at least one scenario where this beats the static top-1 Dijkstra choice.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This is the actual convergence point of Mission 5's two skill folders.
portfolioEvidence.content: This is the actual convergence point of Mission 5's two skill folders — the module the boss battle will extend into the Digital Twin's full grid-routing system.

labs.title: Candidate Routing
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise implementing k-shortest-paths candidate generation combined with a policy-based selection, requiring a fix for a planted single-candidate (k=1) bug.

simulations.title: Dijkstra + RL Candidate Visualizer
simulations.description: Shows Dijkstra generating a candidate path shortlist and an RL policy selecting among them, contrasted with a static top-1-only choice.

flashcards.front: Why restrict an RL agent's routing action space to Dijkstra's top-k candidates instead of every possible path?
flashcards.back: The full space of every possible route is usually far too large for an RL agent to explore directly; letting Dijkstra generate a small set of strong candidates first makes the learning problem tractable.

flashcards.front: What's wrong with a candidate generator that always returns k=1 (only the Dijkstra-optimal path)?
flashcards.back: It gives the RL agent no actual choice to learn from, since there's only ever one option — defeating the entire purpose of combining a learned policy with the graph algorithm.

rubric: Candidate generator produces a genuine set of multiple distinct, reasonable route options, not a single default
Constructed scenario clearly demonstrates a case where the adaptive policy would plausibly outperform a static choice
Correctly explains the practical rationale for restricting rather than eliminating the graph algorithm's role
